title: My grandfather's last pair of shoes
summary: A woman in Lisbon inherits her grandfather's tiny shoe-repair stall and slowly understands why customers came to him, even when faster and cheaper repairs were available nearby.
body:
When my grandfather died, he left me his flat, his radio, and a thing nobody wanted — a shoe-repair stall the size of a cupboard, wedged between a pharmacy and a bank in Lisbon's Graça district. My cousins told me to sell the lease. I am a graphic designer; I had never held a cobbler's hammer. But I could not bring myself to empty it, so for one month I opened it as he had, to understand what I was throwing away.

The first week was humiliating. A man brought in boots with a torn seam, and I sewed them so badly he came back next day for his money. An old woman left a shoe with a broken heel, and I did not even know how my grandfather had charged: by the job, by the difficulty, by the customer? There was no price list. He had carried it all in his head.

What saved me was a notebook under the till. It was not a book of accounts. It was a book of people. 'Senhora Pinto — left shoe always wears first, she favours that leg since the fall. Mr Costa — will say the price is too high, then pay it and tip. The teacher — bring her work forward, she has no other shoes.' Forty years of customers, written as people, not as a transaction. He had not been repairing shoes; he had been keeping a small, accurate map of a neighbourhood's feet.

There is a chain repair shop two streets down, faster and cheaper than I will ever be, with a machine that resoles a shoe in eleven minutes. I went in expecting to feel defeated, and felt something stranger. The young man was perfectly polite, but he did not look at the shoes as they came in. He looked at the ticket. He could not have told you that the left one always wears first, because to him there was no Senhora Pinto — only a job number and a price.

I have kept the stall open for seven months now, which was not the plan. I am still slow. But I have started my own notebook, and noticed something my grandfather never said. People do not bring me their shoes because I am the best repairer in Lisbon; I am plainly not. They bring them because when they sit on the little stool and tell me their foot has been hurting, I am repairing a foot that belongs to a person I am coming to know. The chain shop repairs shoes. I am inheriting a relationship, one resole at a time.

I will probably never earn what my cousins thought I was wasting, and one day a higher rent or a tired knee will close the stall. But I no longer think he left it to me by accident. I think he knew which grandchild could not throw away a notebook full of strangers, and left the stall to the person, not the trade.

annotations.meaningVi: đành lòng / nỡ làm điều gì
sổ kế toán / sổ thu chi
bà ấy dồn trọng lực sang chân đó (giữ ý cái chân đó)
thừa kế một mối quan hệ

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect (recent past with present result) + 'used to' implied through habit
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nối quá khứ với hiện tại: 'I have kept the stall open now for seven months', 'I have started my own notebook', 'I have noticed something', 'I am slowly coming to know'. Lưu ý đối lập giữa thì hiện tại đơn cho thói quen lặp lại ('left shoe always wears first', 'he will say the price is too high') và hiện tại hoàn thành cho kết quả tích lũy đến giờ. 'I had never held a cobbler's hammer' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành để chỉ kinh nghiệm (không có) trước một mốc quá khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The essay uses the present perfect to link past to present: 'I have kept the stall open now for seven months', 'I have started my own notebook', 'I have noticed something'. Note the contrast between the present simple for repeated habit ('left shoe always wears first', 'he will say the price is too high') and the present perfect for a result built up until now. 'I had never held a cobbler's hammer' uses the past perfect for (lack of) experience before a past point.

translationVi:
Khi ông tôi mất, ông để lại cho tôi căn hộ, cái radio, và một thứ không ai muốn — một quầy sửa giày to bằng cái tủ, kẹp giữa một hiệu thuốc và một ngân hàng ở khu Graça của Lisbon. Các anh chị họ bảo tôi bán lại hợp đồng thuê. Tôi là một nhà thiết kế đồ họa; tôi chưa từng cầm búa của thợ sửa giày. Nhưng tôi không nỡ dọn trống nó, nên trong một tháng tôi mở nó như ông từng làm, để hiểu tôi đang vứt đi cái gì.

Tuần đầu tiên thật bẽ bàng. Một người đàn ông mang đến đôi bốt bị bục đường chỉ, và tôi khâu tệ đến mức hôm sau ông quay lại lấy tiền. Một bà cụ để lại một chiếc giày gót gãy, và tôi còn chẳng biết ông tính tiền thế nào: theo việc, theo độ khó, hay theo khách? Không có bảng giá nào. Ông giữ tất cả trong đầu.

Thứ cứu tôi là một cuốn sổ dưới ngăn đựng tiền. Nó không phải sổ thu chi. Nó là một cuốn sổ về con người. 'Bà Pinto — giày trái luôn mòn trước, bà giữ ý chân đó từ sau cú ngã. Ông Costa — sẽ nói giá cao quá, rồi trả và còn cho thêm. Cô giáo — làm sớm cho cô, cô không có đôi nào khác.' Bốn mươi năm khách hàng, được ghi như những con người, không phải những giao dịch. Ông không phải đang sửa giày; ông đang giữ một tấm bản đồ nhỏ, chính xác, về bàn chân của cả một khu phố.

Có một cửa hàng sửa giày dây chuyền cách đó hai phố, nhanh hơn và rẻ hơn tôi nhiều, với một cái máy thay đế giày trong mười một phút. Tôi vào với ý nghĩ sẽ thấy mình thất bại, và lại thấy một điều lạ hơn. Cậu thanh niên hoàn toàn lịch sự, nhưng cậu không nhìn những đôi giày khi chúng được đưa vào. Cậu nhìn cái phiếu. Cậu không thể nói cho bạn rằng chiếc trái luôn mòn trước, vì với cậu không có bà Pinto nào — chỉ có một mã số công việc và một cái giá.

Tôi đã mở quầy được bảy tháng rồi, vốn không phải kế hoạch. Tôi vẫn chậm. Nhưng tôi đã bắt đầu cuốn sổ riêng, và nhận ra một điều ông tôi chưa bao giờ nói ra. Người ta không mang giày đến cho tôi vì tôi là thợ giỏi nhất Lisbon; rõ ràng tôi không phải. Họ mang đến vì khi họ ngồi trên cái ghế đẩu nhỏ và kể rằng chân họ đau, tôi đang sửa một bàn chân thuộc về một con người mà tôi đang dần quen biết. Cửa hàng dây chuyền sửa giày. Tôi đang thừa kế một mối quan hệ, mỗi lần thay đế một.

Tôi có lẽ sẽ không bao giờ kiếm được số tiền mà các anh chị họ nghĩ tôi đang phí, và một ngày nào đó một mức thuê cao hơn hay một đầu gối mỏi mệt sẽ đóng cái quầy. Nhưng tôi không còn nghĩ ông để lại nó cho tôi một cách tình cờ. Tôi nghĩ ông biết đứa cháu nào không nỡ vứt đi một cuốn sổ đầy người lạ, và đã để lại cái quầy cho con người, không phải cái nghề.

questions.prompt: What did the grandfather leave the narrator that nobody wanted?
options: A shoe-repair stall the size of a cupboard, wedged between a pharmacy and a bank in Graça
questions.explanation: 'a shoe-repair stall the size of a cupboard, wedged between a pharmacy and a bank on a steep street in the Graça district.'

questions.prompt: Why did she open the stall instead of selling the lease?
options: She could not bring herself to empty it, and wanted to understand what she was throwing away
She had always wanted to be a cobbler
questions.explanation: 'I could not bring myself to empty it, so for one month I decided to open it as he had, just to understand what I was throwing away.'

questions.prompt: What two problems showed up in the first week?
options: She sewed a torn seam so badly a customer came back, and she did not know how her grandfather had charged
The stall flooded and the lock broke
Thieves came and the radio died
Her cousins reported her to the bank
questions.explanation: The man with the torn seam got his money back; 'I did not know how my grandfather had charged... There was no price list.'

questions.prompt: What was the notebook under the till actually?
options: A book of people — notes on each customer's feet, habits and circumstances, not accounts
A book of debts owed to him
questions.explanation: 'It was not a book of accounts. It was a book of people.'

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say her grandfather was really keeping?
options: A small, accurate map of a neighbourhood's feet
questions.explanation: 'He had been keeping a small, accurate map of a neighbourhood's feet.'

questions.prompt: What did she notice about the young man at the chain shop?
options: He was polite but looked at the ticket, not the shoes — to him there was a job number, not a Senhora Pinto
He kept a notebook of people too
questions.explanation: 'he did not look at the shoes as they came in. He looked at the ticket... only a job number and a price.'

questions.prompt: Why does she say people bring her their shoes?
options: Not because she is the best repairer — she is not — but because she is repairing a foot belonging to a person she is coming to know
questions.explanation: 'They bring them because... I am repairing a foot that belongs to a person I am slowly coming to know.'

questions.prompt: What is the contrast she draws in 'The chain shop repairs shoes. I am inheriting a relationship'?
options: The chain handles transactions; she has taken on the ongoing human bond her grandfather built
The chain is dishonest while she is honest
She repairs shoes and they repair relationships
questions.explanation: 'The chain shop repairs shoes. I am inheriting a relationship, one resole at a time.'

questions.prompt: What does she expect the stall's future to be, financially?
options: It will not make her rich; one day higher rent or a tired knee will close it for good
It will become a national chain
It will be sold to her cousins
questions.explanation: 'The stall will not make me rich, and one day a higher rent or a tired knee will close it for good.'

questions.prompt: What does she conclude about why her grandfather left her the stall?
options: He knew which grandchild could not throw away a notebook full of strangers, and left the stall to the person, not the trade
He wanted to punish his other grandchildren
He hoped she would sell it
questions.explanation: 'he left the stall to the person, not to the trade.'

questions.prompt: In context, 'I could not bring myself to empty it' means she could not do what?
options: Force herself to clear the stall out
questions.explanation: 'bring myself to' means to make oneself do something unwelcome — here, emptying the stall.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the essay?
options: A faster, cheaper service can repair the object while missing the person; what the narrator inherited was not a trade but the relationships her grandfather had patiently kept.
Hand repair is always better than machine repair.
Family businesses should always be sold.
Cobbling is an easy job to learn.
questions.explanation: 'I am inheriting a relationship... he left the stall to the person, not to the trade.'

blanks.explanation: A stall the size of a cupboard.
My cousins told me to sell the lease.
A notebook found under the till.
It was a book of people.
Written as a person, not as a transaction.
A machine that resoles a shoe in eleven minutes.
He looked at the ticket, not the shoes.
They sit on the little stool.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The grandfather's notebook recorded people, not prices. Think of a job you have seen done — a barber, a doctor, a teacher — where the real skill is knowing the person, not just performing the task. What would be lost if that job were measured only by speed and price?
